learning_objectives: Set up a Python development environment with Anaconda and Jupyter
Write and execute basic Python programs using variables and operators
Understand why Python is the dominant language in health informatics
readings: Why Python for Healthcare Data Science (article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Programming is an essential skill for health informatics professionals. Python has emerged as the language of choice for healthcare data analysis, automation, and system integration because of its readable syntax, rich data science ecosystem, and dedicated libraries for healthcare standards like FHIR. This course teaches programming through healthcare-relevant examples so that every concept you learn is immediately applicable to your future career. By the end of this semester, you will be able to read patient data files, analyze clinical datasets, interact with healthcare APIs, build data pipelines, and produce professional clinical reports. No prior programming experience is expected, but intellectual curiosity about both technology and healthcare is required.
key_concepts: Why Python for health informatics: readability, data science libraries (pandas, numpy, scipy), FHIR client libraries, and a massive community of healthcare developers
Development environment: Anaconda distribution bundles Python with 250+ scientific packages; Jupyter notebooks mix code, output, and narrative for reproducible analysis
Variables and assignment: names that store values; patient_age = 72 stores the integer 72 in a variable called patient_age
Data types: strings for patient names, integers for counts, floats for lab values, booleans for clinical flags like is_diabetic
Basic operators: arithmetic (+, -, *, /, //, %, **), comparison (==, !=, <, >), logical (and, or, not)
Input/output: print() displays results, input() reads user text, f-strings format output with embedded variables
Comments and documentation: single-line comments with # explain why, not what; docstrings document functions
The Python REPL and Jupyter cell execution model: immediate feedback for learning and exploration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a program that calculates Body Mass Index (BMI) from height and weight inputs, classifies the result using WHO categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese), and formats the output as a clinical note with patient demographics and interpretation

review.value: High-level programming language known for readable syntax and extensive libraries for data analysis (pandas), statistics (scipy), machine learning (scikit-learn), and healthcare interoperability (fhirclient)
Interactive coding environment mixing code cells, output, and markdown documentation in a single document; ideal for data exploration, analysis workflows, and creating reproducible clinical reports
A named container for data: patient_name = 'Jane Doe' stores the string in a variable you can reference later; variable names should be descriptive and use snake_case in Python
Formatted string literal: f'Patient {name} has BMI {bmi:.1f}' embeds variables and formatting directly into output strings; the :.1f formats a float to one decimal place

class_schedule.topic: Control Flow and Clinical Decision Logic
learning_objectives: Write conditional statements for clinical classification and decision support
Implement loops for processing collections of patient records
Define and call functions for reusable clinical calculations

lecture_notes.introduction: Control flow and functions are the building blocks of useful programs. Clinical decision support systems are built on exactly these constructs: if/elif/else chains that classify patient data, loops that process entire patient populations, and functions that encapsulate reusable clinical logic. Today we build the skills to implement all three through healthcare examples including blood pressure classification, A1c interpretation, and batch processing of patient records. The programming patterns you learn today will recur in every subsequent week of this course and throughout your career in health informatics. By the end of this session, you will have written a function that any clinician could use to classify blood pressure readings according to the latest guidelines.
key_concepts: Conditional statements: if/elif/else for clinical decision logic; each branch handles a different clinical classification
Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >= for evaluating clinical thresholds like A1c >= 6.5
Logical operators: and, or, not for combining conditions like systolic > 130 and diastolic > 80
For loops: iterating over patient lists with for patient in patients; processing each record identically
While loops: repeating until a condition is met; useful for retry logic and interactive data entry
Range function: range(start, stop, step) generates numeric sequences for indexed iteration
Functions: def classify_bp(systolic, diastolic) defines reusable code with named parameters and a return value
Scope: local variables exist only inside their function; this prevents accidental data leakage between patients
Docstrings: triple-quoted strings at the top of a function document its purpose, parameters, and return value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a function that evaluates blood pressure readings and returns a clinical classification (normal, elevated, stage 1 HTN, stage 2 HTN, hypertensive crisis) based on ACC/AHA guidelines, then use it to process a list of 20 patient readings and produce a summary report
setup_requirements: ACC/AHA blood pressure classification reference table

review.value: if/elif/else statements that evaluate conditions and branch execution; the foundation of clinical decision support logic where different thresholds trigger different classifications
Reusable code blocks that accept inputs (parameters), perform operations, and return outputs; def calculate_gfr(creatinine, age, sex) encapsulates a clinical calculation for reuse
Iterates over a collection: for patient in patient_list processes each patient identically; essential for batch processing clinical data
Variables defined inside a function are local and cannot be accessed outside; this prevents one patient's data from accidentally contaminating another patient's calculation

learning_objectives: Use lists, dictionaries, tuples, and sets to represent healthcare data structures
Manipulate nested data structures to model complex patient records
Choose the appropriate data structure for different healthcare data scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare data naturally fits into Python data structures. A patient record maps to a dictionary with keys like name, date_of_birth, and diagnoses. A series of lab results over time maps to a list. A set of unique medication codes eliminates duplicates from a medication reconciliation. Choosing the right data structure makes your code clearer, faster, and less error-prone. Today we learn to model clinical data in Python and manipulate it programmatically. These are the same data structures that underlie FHIR resources, EHR databases, and clinical research datasets. Mastering them enables you to move fluidly between raw data files, API responses, and analytical workflows without losing information or introducing errors.
key_concepts: Lists: ordered, mutable collections for lab result series, medication lists, and encounter histories; accessed by index
Dictionaries: key-value pairs for patient records, code lookups (ICD-10 to description), and configuration; accessed by key name
Tuples: immutable ordered collections for vital sign snapshots (120, 80, 'mmHg') where values should not change after recording
Sets: unordered unique collections for medication reconciliation, unique diagnosis codes, and deduplication tasks
List comprehensions: concise filtering and transformation: [p for p in patients if p['age'] > 65] finds elderly patients in one line
Nested data structures: a patient dictionary containing a list of encounters, each with a dictionary of vitals; models real clinical records
Dictionary methods: .get() for safe access with defaults, .keys(), .values(), .items() for iteration
Sorting and searching: sorted() with key functions, list.index(), dictionary membership testing with 'key' in dict
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a patient record as a nested dictionary containing demographics, medication list, allergy list, encounter history (list of dictionaries with vitals), then write functions to search medications, check drug-allergy interactions, and summarize vital sign trends
setup_requirements: Sample patient data in Python dictionary format

review.value: Key-value data structure: {'mrn': '12345', 'name': 'Jane Doe', 'diagnoses': ['E11.9', 'I10']}; the natural Python representation of a patient record or any labeled dataset
Concise syntax for filtering and transforming collections: [p['name'] for p in patients if p['age'] > 65 and 'I10' in p['dx']]; readable and efficient for healthcare data filtering
Dictionaries containing lists containing dictionaries: patient['encounters'][0]['vitals']['bp_systolic']; mirrors the hierarchical structure of real clinical data and FHIR resources
Sets support union, intersection, and difference: current_meds & contraindicated_meds finds dangerous overlaps in a medication reconciliation

learning_objectives: Read and write CSV files containing clinical and administrative health data
Parse JSON data including FHIR resource representations
Implement robust error handling and data validation for health data files
readings: HL7 FHIR Patient Resource JSON specification
Python csv and json module documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics professionals constantly work with data files exported from electronic health records, lab systems, billing platforms, and public health registries. CSV files carry tabular data like lab results, discharge summaries, and billing records. JSON files carry structured data from FHIR APIs, configuration files, and data interchange formats. XML files carry C-CDA clinical documents. Python makes reading, validating, transforming, and writing all of these formats straightforward. The skills you build today transform you from someone who can only work with data in Excel or a proprietary tool to someone who can process any healthcare data file programmatically, at any scale, with full auditability and reproducibility.
key_concepts: File handling fundamentals: open(), read(), write(), close(), and the context manager (with statement) that guarantees file cleanup
CSV reading with csv.reader (positional) and csv.DictReader (named columns): row['patient_id'] is more readable than row[0]
CSV writing with csv.writer and csv.DictWriter for producing reports and transformed datasets
JSON parsing: json.load() reads a file, json.loads() parses a string; both produce Python dictionaries and lists
JSON writing: json.dump() writes to file, json.dumps() produces a string; use indent=2 for human-readable output
FHIR resource navigation: patient_json['name'][0]['family'] extracts the family name from a FHIR Patient resource
Error handling: try/except blocks for FileNotFoundError, json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, and data type mismatches
Data validation: checking required fields, validating date formats, ensuring numeric ranges for lab values
Encoding: UTF-8 for international patient names; specify encoding='utf-8' when opening files
lecture_notes.demonstration: Read a CSV file of lab results (with missing values and format inconsistencies), parse a FHIR Patient resource from JSON, combine data by patient MRN, validate all fields, handle errors gracefully, and produce a summary report CSV
setup_requirements: Sample CSV lab results file with intentional data quality issues

review.value: csv.DictReader reads CSV rows as dictionaries keyed by header names: row['hemoglobin'] instead of row[3]; makes code self-documenting and resilient to column reordering
json.load(open('patient.json')) converts JSON file to Python dictionary; essential for working with FHIR API responses and modern health data interchange

try/except blocks catch and handle errors gracefully: try: value = float(row['glucose']) except ValueError: value = None; critical when processing health data where missing values and format inconsistencies are common
with open('data.csv') as f: guarantees the file is closed even if an error occurs; always use this pattern instead of manual open/close

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to pandas for Healthcare Data Analysis
learning_objectives: Create and manipulate pandas DataFrames from CSV, JSON, and dictionary sources
Perform filtering, grouping, and aggregation on clinical datasets
Handle missing data appropriately in health data contexts

lecture_notes.introduction: pandas is the most important Python library for health data analysis. It provides the DataFrame, a two-dimensional tabular data structure with labeled rows and columns that makes working with healthcare datasets intuitive and powerful. Think of a DataFrame as a programmable spreadsheet that can handle millions of rows, automate complex calculations, and produce publication-quality summaries. pandas integrates seamlessly with visualization libraries (matplotlib, seaborn), statistical tools (scipy, statsmodels), and machine learning frameworks (scikit-learn). For health informatics professionals, pandas replaces the manual Excel workflows that are error-prone, non-reproducible, and limited to small datasets. After today, you will never want to do healthcare data analysis in a spreadsheet again.
key_concepts: DataFrame creation: pd.read_csv(), pd.read_json(), pd.DataFrame(dict_list) convert multiple data sources into analysis-ready tables
Column selection: df['hemoglobin'] for single column, df[['name', 'age', 'diagnosis']] for multiple columns
Boolean indexing: df[df['age'] > 65] filters rows; chainable with & (and) and | (or) operators
Loc and iloc: df.loc[row_label, col_label] for label-based, df.iloc[row_num, col_num] for position-based access
GroupBy aggregation: df.groupby('diagnosis')['los'].mean() calculates average length of stay per diagnosis in one line
Missing data: df.isna().sum() counts missing values per column; df.fillna() and df.dropna() handle them appropriately
Descriptive statistics: df.describe() produces count, mean, std, min, quartiles, max for all numeric columns
Data types: df.dtypes shows column types; df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date']) converts strings to dates
Sorting: df.sort_values('los', ascending=False) orders by length of stay; .head(10) shows the top 10
Apply: df['bmi'] = df.apply(lambda row: row['weight'] / (row['height']**2), axis=1) creates computed columns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Load a hospital discharge dataset (1000+ rows), calculate average length of stay by diagnosis, identify the top 10 diagnoses by volume, compute 30-day readmission rates, find patients with abnormal lab values, and visualize discharge trends over time
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with pandas, matplotlib, seaborn installed

review.value: Two-dimensional tabular data structure with labeled rows and columns; think of it as a programmable spreadsheet optimized for data analysis that handles millions of rows efficiently
Split-apply-combine operation: df.groupby('diagnosis')['los'].agg(['mean', 'count']) groups data, applies functions, and combines results; essential for healthcare quality metrics

Healthcare data always has missing values; pandas represents them as NaN; df.isna().sum() counts them, df.dropna(subset=['critical_field']) removes rows missing critical values while preserving partial data
Filter rows by condition: diabetic_elderly = df[(df['dx'] == 'E11.9') & (df['age'] > 65)]; parentheses required around each condition when combining with & or |

learning_objectives: Create publication-quality charts from healthcare datasets using matplotlib and seaborn
Select appropriate chart types for different clinical data analysis questions
Build multi-panel dashboards summarizing key health metrics
readings: Fundamentals of Data Visualization by Claus Wilke, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective data visualization transforms raw healthcare data into actionable insights. A well-designed chart communicates findings to clinicians, administrators, and policymakers far more effectively than a table of numbers. Python's matplotlib and seaborn libraries provide the tools to create every chart type used in healthcare reporting: bar charts for diagnosis frequencies, line charts for lab value trends, scatter plots for correlation analysis, box plots for distribution comparisons, and heatmaps for correlation matrices. Today we learn to choose the right visualization for each analytical question and to produce charts that meet the standards of clinical publications and quality improvement reports. Every visualization must have clear axis labels, a descriptive title, appropriate color choices, and honest scales.
key_concepts: matplotlib fundamentals: fig, ax = plt.subplots() creates a figure; ax.plot(), ax.bar(), ax.scatter() add data; ax.set_xlabel(), ax.set_title() add labels
Bar charts: categorical comparisons like diagnosis frequency, readmission rates by unit, or average LOS by payer
Line charts: temporal trends like monthly admissions, lab value trajectories, or infection rates over time
Scatter plots: correlations between continuous variables like age vs. length of stay or BMI vs. A1c
Box plots: distribution comparisons across groups like blood pressure by medication type or costs by diagnosis
Histograms: distribution shape of a single variable like patient ages, wait times, or lab values
Seaborn: higher-level interface with built-in statistical visualization; sns.boxplot(), sns.heatmap(), sns.pairplot()
Multi-panel figures: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2) creates a 2x2 dashboard summarizing multiple metrics
Color and accessibility: use colorblind-friendly palettes; avoid red/green only; label data directly when possible
Saving figures: fig.savefig('report.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') for publication-quality output
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a four-panel quality dashboard for a hospital unit: (1) bar chart of top 10 diagnoses, (2) line chart of monthly admission trends, (3) box plot of length of stay by payer type, (4) heatmap of lab value correlations; save as a publication-ready PNG
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with matplotlib, seaborn installed
Color palette reference for healthcare publications

review.value: Comparison across categories: bar chart. Trend over time: line chart. Relationship between variables: scatter plot. Distribution: histogram or box plot. Correlation matrix: heatmap. Always start with the question, then choose the chart type.

Seaborn provides statistical visualizations with less code: sns.boxplot(data=df, x='diagnosis', y='los') vs. several matplotlib calls. Use seaborn for exploration, matplotlib for fine-tuned publication figures.
Every figure needs: descriptive title, labeled axes with units, legend if multiple series, appropriate font size (12pt minimum), and sufficient resolution (300 dpi for print, 150 for screen).
8% of males are red-green colorblind. Use seaborn palettes like 'colorblind' or 'viridis'. Add patterns or labels as secondary encoding. Test with a colorblindness simulator.

class_schedule.topic: APIs and FHIR for Health Data Access
learning_objectives: Make HTTP requests to healthcare APIs using the Python requests library
Query a FHIR server for patient data, observations, and conditions
Parse and analyze FHIR Bundle responses containing multiple clinical resources
readings: FHIR RESTful API Quick Start Guide (hl7.org)

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern health data access is API-driven. The HL7 FHIR standard is rapidly becoming the universal interface for accessing patient data from electronic health records, health information exchanges, and public health systems. The 21st Century Cures Act mandates FHIR APIs for patient data access, making this skill legally required for health IT systems. Learning to interact with FHIR APIs programmatically is one of the most marketable skills in health informatics today. With Python and the requests library, you can query any FHIR-compliant system, retrieve patient demographics, lab results, medications, conditions, and encounters, then feed that data into your pandas analysis pipelines. This session bridges the gap between the file-based analysis you have done so far and the real-time API-driven workflows used in production health IT systems.
key_concepts: HTTP fundamentals: GET retrieves data, POST creates data; status codes 200 (success), 404 (not found), 401 (unauthorized)
Python requests library: response = requests.get(url, headers=headers, params=params) makes API calls clean and simple
FHIR RESTful operations: GET /Patient/123 reads a patient; GET /Patient?name=Smith searches; GET /Observation?code=4548-4 finds A1c results
FHIR search parameters: _count for pagination, _include for related resources, _sort for ordering
FHIR Bundle resources: search results return a Bundle containing an 'entry' array with individual resources
Parsing FHIR responses: navigating nested JSON to extract clinical values like observation.valueQuantity.value
Authentication: OAuth2 and SMART on FHIR for authorized access to patient data
Rate limiting and pagination: handling APIs that return partial results requiring follow-up requests
Error handling: checking status codes, handling timeouts, retrying transient failures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Connect to the HAPI FHIR public test server, search for patients with a diabetes diagnosis, retrieve their A1c lab results, load everything into a pandas DataFrame, calculate A1c trends per patient, and visualize the results
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with requests library installed

review.value: GET /Patient?name=Smith or GET /Observation?code=4548-4 (A1c) are standard URL patterns for querying FHIR servers; search parameters narrow results without downloading entire databases
Container resource returned by FHIR search containing an 'entry' array; iterate through bundle['entry'] to access each result's 'resource' object for processing
response = requests.get(url, params={'code': '4548-4'}); response.json() parses the JSON body; response.status_code checks for errors; the standard Python HTTP client
OAuth2-based authorization framework for FHIR APIs; applications request specific scopes (patient/*.read) and receive access tokens; required for production EHR integration

class_schedule.topic: Clinical Data Cleaning and Quality Assessment
learning_objectives: Identify and categorize common data quality issues in healthcare datasets
Implement systematic data cleaning pipelines using pandas
Produce data quality assessment reports documenting issues and remediation
readings: Python for Data Analysis Ch. 7 (Data Cleaning)
Healthcare Data Quality: A Systematic Review (journal article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare data is notoriously messy. Missing lab values, inconsistent date formats, duplicate patient records, impossible vital signs (blood pressure of 0/0 or 999/999), free-text entries where coded values were expected, and mismatched units are routine problems in every healthcare dataset. A data analysis is only as trustworthy as the data it uses, and publishing results from uncleaned healthcare data can lead to incorrect clinical decisions. Today we build systematic data cleaning pipelines that identify, document, and remediate quality issues reproducibly. The pipeline approach ensures that every cleaning step is auditable, reversible, and applicable to future datasets from the same source. Data quality assessment is not a one-time task; it is a continuous process that should run every time data is ingested.
key_concepts: Data quality dimensions: completeness (missing values), accuracy (correct values), consistency (matching across sources), timeliness, and validity (within expected ranges)
Missing data patterns: MCAR (random), MAR (related to observed data), MNAR (related to the missing value itself); each requires different handling
Duplicate detection: df.duplicated(subset=['mrn', 'encounter_date']) finds duplicate records; deduplication strategies depend on context
Outlier detection: vital signs outside physiological ranges (systolic BP < 50 or > 300) are data entry errors, not clinical outliers
Date standardization: pd.to_datetime() handles multiple formats; always convert to a standard format immediately upon loading
Code standardization: mapping free-text diagnoses to ICD-10 codes; using lookup dictionaries for consistent coding
Data type enforcement: ensuring numeric columns are numeric, dates are datetime, and categorical columns use consistent values
Pipeline construction: chain cleaning steps in a function that takes raw DataFrame input and returns cleaned output with a quality report
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a raw hospital dataset with 15 intentional data quality issues (missing values, impossible vital signs, duplicate records, inconsistent date formats, free-text where codes are expected), build a cleaning pipeline that fixes each issue, and produce a data quality report showing before/after statistics

review.value: A function that takes raw data and returns cleaned data plus a report: def clean_vitals(df): applies range checks, handles missing values, standardizes units, and logs every change for auditability
Heart rate 30-220, systolic BP 50-300, temperature 90-110F, SpO2 0-100; values outside these ranges are data errors, not clinical findings; flag and investigate rather than silently removing

Never blindly drop or fill missing values; document the pattern (which columns, which patients, when), determine the mechanism (random vs systematic), then choose: drop, fill with median, forward-fill for time series, or flag as unknown
Every cleaning step must be logged: what was changed, why, how many records affected; this audit trail is required for regulatory compliance and reproducibility

learning_objectives: Perform descriptive and inferential statistical analysis on clinical datasets
Apply hypothesis testing to compare clinical outcomes between groups
Interpret statistical results in the context of clinical significance
readings: Biostatistics for the Health Sciences (selected chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistical analysis transforms healthcare data into evidence. Descriptive statistics summarize what happened; inferential statistics tell you whether differences are meaningful or due to chance. Health informatics professionals must be able to compute and interpret both. Python's scipy library provides every statistical test you need for clinical data analysis, and pandas makes applying them to large datasets efficient. Today we go beyond df.describe() to perform real hypothesis testing: is the difference in length of stay between two treatment groups statistically significant? Is there a correlation between BMI and A1c? Is the readmission rate for one unit significantly higher than the hospital average? These are the questions that drive quality improvement, clinical research, and health policy.
key_concepts: Descriptive statistics: mean, median, mode, standard deviation, IQR, percentiles; df.describe() and df.agg()
Distributions: normal distribution for many biological measurements; skewed distributions for length of stay and costs
Hypothesis testing framework: null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, significance level (alpha=0.05), p-value interpretation
t-tests: comparing means between two groups; scipy.stats.ttest_ind() for independent samples (treatment vs control)
Chi-squared test: comparing proportions; scipy.stats.chi2_contingency() for readmission rates by unit or complication rates by procedure
Correlation: pearson for linear, spearman for monotonic; df.corr() computes the full correlation matrix
ANOVA: comparing means across three or more groups; scipy.stats.f_oneway() for LOS by diagnosis category
Confidence intervals: the range where the true population parameter likely falls; more informative than p-values alone
Clinical vs statistical significance: a statistically significant 0.1-day LOS difference may be clinically meaningless
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using the hospital discharge dataset: (1) test whether length of stay differs significantly between surgical and medical patients, (2) test whether readmission rates differ by payer type, (3) compute the correlation between patient age and total charges, (4) interpret all results with attention to clinical significance
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with scipy, pandas, matplotlib

review.value: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the data, assuming the null hypothesis is true; p < 0.05 is the conventional threshold for statistical significance, but always report the actual p-value and effect size
Compares means of two groups: t_stat, p_val = ttest_ind(surgical_los, medical_los); assumes approximately normal distributions and adequate sample sizes

Statistical significance (p < 0.05) does not imply clinical importance; with large sample sizes, trivially small differences become statistically significant. Always report effect size (Cohen's d, odds ratio) alongside p-values.
A strong correlation between two variables does not prove one causes the other; confounding variables may explain the association. Always look for plausible causal mechanisms.

class_schedule.topic: Health Data Standards: ICD-10, LOINC, SNOMED, and HL7
learning_objectives: Work with ICD-10, LOINC, and SNOMED CT code sets programmatically in Python
Map between clinical terminologies and aggregate data by code hierarchies
Parse HL7v2 messages and understand their role in health system integration
readings: Health Informatics: An Interprofessional Approach, Ch. on Standards

lecture_notes.introduction: Healthcare data interoperability depends on standardized code systems. ICD-10 codes classify diagnoses and procedures for billing and epidemiology. LOINC codes identify laboratory tests and clinical observations. SNOMED CT provides a comprehensive clinical terminology for EHR documentation. HL7v2 messages carry data between hospital systems in a pipe-delimited format. As a health informatics programmer, you need to work with all of these code systems: parsing coded data, mapping between systems, aggregating by code hierarchies, and validating that codes are used correctly. Today we learn to handle healthcare terminology data in Python, building tools that work with real clinical codes rather than free-text descriptions.
key_concepts: ICD-10-CM structure: category (3 chars), etiology/site (4-5 chars), extension (6-7 chars); E11.65 is type 2 diabetes with hyperglycemia
ICD-10 hierarchy: E11 (type 2 diabetes) is the parent of E11.0-E11.9; grouping by parent code aggregates related conditions
LOINC codes: identify lab tests and observations; 4548-4 is hemoglobin A1c, 2345-7 is glucose; essential for FHIR Observation resources
SNOMED CT: clinical terminology with relationships; 73211009 is diabetes mellitus; supports clinical reasoning and decision support
HL7v2 message parsing: pipe-delimited segments (MSH, PID, OBR, OBX); Python string splitting and segment identification
Code validation: checking that ICD-10 codes are valid and current; rejecting deprecated or malformed codes
Terminology mapping: crosswalks between ICD-10, SNOMED, and local codes using lookup tables
Hierarchical aggregation: grouping ICD-10 codes by chapter (I00-I99 for circulatory) or category (I10-I16 for hypertensive diseases)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Parse a batch of HL7v2 ADT messages to extract patient demographics and diagnosis codes, validate ICD-10 codes against a reference table, group admissions by ICD-10 chapter, and produce a diagnosis frequency report with both code and description

review.value: ICD-10 codes are hierarchical: chapter (I, II, ...) -> block (I10-I16) -> category (I10) -> subcategory (I10.0); aggregating at different levels answers different questions (all hypertension vs specific types)

Universal identifier for laboratory and clinical observations: 4548-4 uniquely identifies hemoglobin A1c regardless of which lab performed the test; required in FHIR Observation resources for interoperability
Segments separated by \r, fields by |, components by ^: PID|1||MRN12345^^^Hospital||SMITH^JOHN||19550315|M; Python string operations parse this structured text format
Different systems use different code sets; mapping ICD-10 E11.9 to SNOMED 44054006 (type 2 diabetes) enables data aggregation across systems using different terminologies

learning_objectives: Design and implement multi-step data processing pipelines for healthcare data
Apply extract-transform-load patterns to clinical data integration scenarios
Build automated workflows that process incoming data files and produce reports
readings: ETL Best Practices for Healthcare Data (whitepaper)
Python pathlib and os module documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Real-world health informatics work rarely involves a single analysis on a single file. Instead, you build data pipelines: automated workflows that extract data from multiple sources, transform it through cleaning and enrichment steps, and load it into analysis-ready datasets or reporting systems. An admission-discharge-transfer pipeline might read HL7 messages from a feed, parse patient demographics and diagnoses, validate codes, merge with lab results from a FHIR API, compute quality metrics, and produce a daily dashboard. Each step must handle errors, log its actions, and produce auditable output. Today we combine all the skills from previous weeks into complete, production-style data pipelines that run reliably without manual intervention.
key_concepts: ETL pattern: Extract (read from source), Transform (clean, validate, enrich), Load (write to destination); the backbone of healthcare data integration
Pipeline architecture: modular functions for each step; each function takes a DataFrame input and returns a DataFrame output
File system operations: pathlib for finding input files, creating output directories, and managing file paths across platforms
Logging: Python logging module for recording pipeline progress, warnings, and errors; essential for debugging automated workflows
Configuration: separating parameters (file paths, thresholds, code mappings) from code using config files or environment variables
Scheduling: running pipelines automatically on a schedule; cron jobs, Windows Task Scheduler, or Python schedule library
Idempotency: running the pipeline twice with the same input produces the same output; essential for reliability
Error recovery: checkpoint files that allow a failed pipeline to resume from where it stopped rather than restarting from scratch
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a complete daily discharge summary pipeline: (1) scan an input directory for new CSV discharge files, (2) read and validate each file, (3) clean data and standardize codes, (4) merge with a reference diagnosis table, (5) compute quality metrics (average LOS, readmission flags), (6) produce a summary report, (7) archive processed files, (8) log all steps
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook and Python script environment

review.value: Extract-Transform-Load: read raw data from source systems, apply cleaning and transformation logic, write the result to a destination; the standard pattern for all healthcare data integration
Each pipeline step is a function: def validate_vitals(df) -> df, def map_icd_codes(df, lookup) -> df; this makes pipelines testable, debuggable, and reusable
import logging; logger.info(f'Processed {len(df)} records from {filename}'); production pipelines must log every step so failures can be diagnosed without re-running
A pipeline is idempotent if running it twice produces the same result; achieved by using deterministic logic and tracking which files have already been processed

class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Health Data Databases and SQL
learning_objectives: Write SQL queries to extract data from relational healthcare databases
Use Python to connect to databases and execute queries programmatically
Understand healthcare database schemas and common query patterns

lecture_notes.introduction: Most healthcare data ultimately lives in relational databases. Electronic health records, billing systems, lab information systems, and registries all store data in tables with rows and columns connected by relationships. SQL (Structured Query Language) is the universal language for querying these databases. Python connects to databases using libraries like sqlite3, psycopg2, and SQLAlchemy, enabling you to combine the query power of SQL with the analysis power of pandas. Health informatics professionals who can write SQL queries directly access the data they need without waiting for IT to produce extracts. Common healthcare data models like OMOP CDM provide standardized database schemas that make analyses portable across institutions.
key_concepts: SQL SELECT: SELECT patient_id, name, diagnosis FROM patients WHERE age > 65 retrieves specific columns with conditions
JOINs: combining tables; SELECT p.name, e.encounter_date FROM patients p JOIN encounters e ON p.patient_id = e.patient_id links patients to their encounters
Aggregation: SELECT diagnosis, COUNT(*), AVG(los) FROM discharges GROUP BY diagnosis HAVING COUNT(*) > 10 summarizes data
Subqueries: SELECT * FROM patients WHERE patient_id IN (SELECT patient_id FROM labs WHERE test = 'A1c' AND value > 9.0) finds patients by lab criteria
Python database connection: conn = sqlite3.connect('health.db'); df = pd.read_sql_query(query, conn) loads SQL results directly into pandas
SQLAlchemy: ORM that provides database-agnostic access; same Python code works with SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL
Healthcare database schemas: patient, encounter, observation, condition, medication tables mirror FHIR resources
OMOP CDM: standardized schema used by OHDSI for multi-site clinical research; person, condition_occurrence, drug_exposure tables
lecture_notes.demonstration: Create a SQLite database with patient, encounter, and lab tables; populate with sample data; write SQL queries to find diabetic patients with poor A1c control, calculate readmission rates by diagnosis, and load results into pandas for visualization

review.value: df = pd.read_sql_query('SELECT * FROM patients WHERE age > 65', conn) combines SQL's query efficiency with pandas' analysis power; SQL filters at the database level before data enters Python
Connects related tables: patients JOIN encounters ON patient_id links each patient to their visits; INNER JOIN returns only matched rows; LEFT JOIN keeps all patients even without encounters
GROUP BY diagnosis aggregates rows; HAVING COUNT(*) > 10 filters groups after aggregation; essential for quality metrics like readmission rates by diagnosis

Standardized database schema for clinical research: same table structure across institutions enables multi-site studies without per-site data reformatting; the standard for observational health data science

class_schedule.topic: Healthcare Data Privacy and Security in Code
learning_objectives: Implement HIPAA Safe Harbor de-identification in Python data pipelines
Apply secure coding practices when handling protected health information
Build privacy-preserving data analysis workflows
readings: HIPAA Privacy Rule Safe Harbor De-identification Standard
HHS Guidance on De-identification of PHI

lecture_notes.introduction: Every health informatics professional who touches patient data must understand and implement data privacy protections. HIPAA requires that protected health information (PHI) be safeguarded at every stage: in storage, in transit, and during analysis. The Safe Harbor method specifies 18 identifiers that must be removed or generalized for data to be considered de-identified. As a programmer, you are responsible for ensuring that your code does not leak PHI through log files, error messages, temporary files, or careless variable handling. Today we learn to build privacy-preserving data pipelines that de-identify data correctly, handle PHI securely in memory and on disk, and produce analysis results that can be shared without violating patient privacy. This is not optional compliance overhead; it is a core professional responsibility.
key_concepts: HIPAA Safe Harbor: 18 identifiers to remove or generalize (names, dates, zip codes, MRNs, SSNs, etc.)
De-identification in pandas: replacing names with pseudonyms, generalizing dates to year, truncating zip codes to 3 digits
Date shifting: adding a random but consistent offset to all dates for a patient; preserves intervals while hiding actual dates
Hashing for pseudonymization: hashlib.sha256(mrn.encode()).hexdigest() creates a consistent pseudonym from an MRN
Secure file handling: encrypting PHI at rest, securely deleting temporary files, avoiding PHI in log messages
Minimum necessary principle: only access and process the PHI elements required for the specific analysis task
Audit logging: recording who accessed what data, when, and why; required for HIPAA compliance
De-identified datasets: once properly de-identified, data is no longer PHI and can be shared for research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a patient dataset with full PHI, build a de-identification pipeline that applies Safe Harbor rules (remove names, generalize dates, truncate zip codes, hash MRNs), verify completeness of de-identification, and produce a research-ready dataset with an audit log

review.value: Remove or generalize 18 identifier types: names, geographic data smaller than state, dates (except year), phone numbers, fax numbers, email, SSN, MRN, health plan numbers, account numbers, license numbers, vehicle IDs, device IDs, URLs, IPs, biometric IDs, photos, and any other unique identifier
Add a random offset (e.g., -180 to +180 days) consistently for each patient; preserves the interval between a patient's events (admission to discharge = 5 days) while hiding the actual calendar dates
Never log patient names, MRNs, or other PHI: logger.info(f'Processing patient {mrn}') is a HIPAA violation; use logger.info(f'Processing record {record_index}') instead
Only load the columns you need: pd.read_csv('patients.csv', usecols=['age', 'diagnosis', 'los']) avoids loading PHI columns that are not required for the analysis

class_schedule.topic: Advanced pandas and Reporting for Health Informatics
learning_objectives: Apply advanced pandas techniques including pivot tables, merging strategies, and time series analysis
Generate automated reports from healthcare datasets using Python
Build reusable analysis templates for common healthcare reporting needs

lecture_notes.introduction: Health informatics professionals are frequently asked to produce recurring reports: monthly quality dashboards, quarterly infection surveillance summaries, annual utilization reports, and ad hoc analyses for clinical leadership. Advanced pandas techniques enable you to build these reports as automated, reproducible Python scripts rather than manual spreadsheet processes. Pivot tables reshape data for cross-tabulation reporting. Multi-index DataFrames organize hierarchical results. Time series analysis reveals seasonal patterns in admissions and outcomes. Merge strategies combine data from multiple sources reliably. Today we bring all of your pandas skills together to build a complete automated reporting system that produces the same analyses clinical leaders need, every time, without manual effort.
key_concepts: Pivot tables: pd.pivot_table(df, values='los', index='diagnosis', columns='quarter', aggfunc='mean') creates cross-tabulated summaries
Multi-level index: hierarchical row/column labels for organized reporting; df.set_index(['department', 'diagnosis']).sort_index()
Merge strategies: pd.merge(left, right, on='patient_id', how='left') combines DataFrames; left, right, inner, outer joins for different scenarios
Time series: df.set_index('date').resample('M').count() aggregates by month; .rolling(7).mean() computes moving averages
String operations: df['dx_code'].str[:3] extracts ICD-10 category; .str.contains('E11') finds diabetes codes
Automated report generation: functions that take a date range and produce a complete summary with statistics and visualizations
Export formats: df.to_excel() for stakeholders, df.to_csv() for data exchange, fig.savefig() for embedded charts
Report templates: parameterized functions that generate standard reports for any unit, time period, or diagnosis category
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build an automated monthly quality dashboard that: (1) reads the latest discharge data, (2) computes key metrics (volume, LOS, readmissions, mortality) by department, (3) creates a pivot table comparing current month to previous month, (4) flags departments with metrics outside control limits, (5) generates a multi-page Excel report with charts
setup_requirements: Jupyter notebook with pandas, openpyxl, matplotlib

review.value: Reshapes data for cross-tabulation: rows are diagnoses, columns are months, values are average LOS; the healthcare equivalent of Excel pivot tables but reproducible and automatable

pd.merge(patients, labs, on='mrn', how='left') combines patient demographics with lab results; left join keeps all patients even those without labs; always verify merge results with .shape and spot checks
df.resample('W').sum() aggregates daily data to weekly totals; 'M' for monthly, 'Q' for quarterly; essential for trend analysis in surveillance and quality reporting
A function that takes parameters (start_date, end_date, department) and produces a complete report; run monthly via cron job; stakeholders receive consistent, accurate reports without analyst effort

class_schedule.topic: Course Integration and Final Project Presentations
learning_objectives: Present a complete health data analysis project demonstrating all course skills
Evaluate peer projects and provide constructive technical feedback
Reflect on the full programming toolkit acquired for health informatics practice
readings: Course materials review for final exam preparation

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together everything you have learned across the semester. You have progressed from writing your first print() statement to building automated data pipelines that read healthcare files, clean and validate data, query FHIR APIs, perform statistical analysis, produce visualizations, de-identify PHI, and generate professional reports. Today you present your final projects to demonstrate this growth. Each project applies the full programming toolkit to a real health informatics problem: clinical quality analysis, public health surveillance, patient cohort identification, operational efficiency analysis, or research data preparation. Your presentations should tell a clear story: what healthcare question you investigated, how you built the technical solution, what the data revealed, and what clinical or operational action your analysis supports.
key_concepts: Project presentation structure: problem statement, data sources, methodology, technical implementation, results, clinical interpretation, limitations
Code quality review: clean code, meaningful variable names, functions for reusable logic, error handling, documentation
Reproducibility: another analyst should be able to run your notebook and get the same results with different data from the same source
Clinical interpretation: translating statistical findings into language that clinicians and administrators can act on
Limitations and future work: honest assessment of what the analysis does and does not show; suggestions for extending the work
Portfolio development: your final project becomes a work sample demonstrating health informatics programming competence
Course skill inventory: file I/O, data structures, pandas, visualization, APIs, SQL, statistics, privacy, pipelines, reporting
Continuing education: Python for healthcare resources, AMIA conferences, health informatics certification preparation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor demonstrates the evaluation rubric with a sample project, showing how technical implementation, clinical relevance, code quality, and presentation clarity are assessed
setup_requirements: Student projects loaded and ready for presentation

review.value: A reproducible analysis includes: the raw data (or access instructions), the complete code, all dependencies listed, and clear execution instructions; anyone should be able to verify your results

The best health informatics analyses answer a specific clinical or operational question and suggest a concrete action: 'Unit 4B has a readmission rate 2.3x the hospital average, driven by CHF patients lacking discharge follow-up appointments'

Your final project demonstrates to employers: Python proficiency, healthcare domain knowledge, data analysis skills, visualization ability, and professional communication; keep it polished and accessible

Python fundamentals, data structures, file I/O (CSV, JSON, HL7), pandas analysis, visualization, FHIR APIs, SQL databases, statistics, data quality, privacy/de-identification, pipelines, automated reporting

quizzes.topic: Python Fundamentals, Control Flow, and Data Structures

questions.question: Write a Python function called classify_a1c that takes an A1c value (float) as input and returns a string classification: 'Normal' for A1c < 5.7, 'Pre-diabetes' for 5.7 <= A1c < 6.5, 'Diabetes' for A1c >= 6.5. Include input validation that raises a ValueError if the input is negative or greater than 20.

rubric.key: Correct function definition with parameter
Correct classification thresholds and boundary handling
Input validation with ValueError for out-of-range values
Clean code style and correct return values

questions.question: Given a list of patient dictionaries, write code using a list comprehension to find all patients over age 65 with a diagnosis of hypertension (ICD-10 code starting with 'I10') and return their names and ages as a list of tuples.
questions.answer:
patients = [
    {'name': 'John Smith', 'age': 72, 'diagnoses': ['I10', 'E11.9']},
    {'name': 'Jane Doe', 'age': 45, 'diagnoses': ['J45.20']},
    {'name': 'Bob Jones', 'age': 68, 'diagnoses': ['I10', 'I25.10']}
]

result = [(p['name'], p['age']) for p in patients
         if p['age'] > 65 and any(dx.startswith('I10') for dx in p['diagnoses'])]
# result: [('John Smith', 72), ('Bob Jones', 68)]

rubric.key: Correct age filtering (> 65)
Correct diagnosis code matching with startswith
Proper list comprehension or equivalent loop
Correct output format as list of tuples

questions.question: Explain the difference between a Python list and a dictionary. Give a healthcare example where each would be the appropriate data structure and explain why.
questions.answer: A list is an ordered collection accessed by integer index, ideal for sequential data like a series of blood pressure readings over time: bp_readings = [120, 118, 125, 122]. A dictionary is a key-value mapping accessed by named keys, ideal for structured records like patient demographics: patient = {'name': 'Jane Doe', 'mrn': '12345', 'age': 72}. Use a list when order matters and items are similar (all the same type of measurement). Use a dictionary when you need to look up values by name rather than position (accessing patient['mrn'] is clearer than patient[1]).

rubric.key: Correct distinction between list and dictionary
Appropriate healthcare example for each
Clear explanation of when to use each

quizzes.topic: File Processing, pandas, and Data Visualization

questions.question: Write Python code using pandas to read a CSV file called 'discharges.csv', calculate the average length of stay (column 'los') grouped by 'primary_diagnosis', filter to diagnoses with at least 10 cases, and display the top 5 diagnoses with the highest average LOS sorted in descending order.
questions.answer:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('discharges.csv')
grouped = df.groupby('primary_diagnosis')['los'].agg(['mean', 'count'])
filtered = grouped[grouped['count'] >= 10]
top5 = filtered.sort_values('mean', ascending=False).head(5)
print(top5)

rubric.key: Correct CSV reading and groupby aggregation
Correct filtering by count >= 10
Correct sorting descending and head(5)
Clean, readable pandas code

questions.question: Write Python code to parse the following FHIR Patient JSON and extract the patient's full name, birth date, and MRN (from the identifier array where system contains 'mrn'). Handle the case where any field might be missing using .get() with defaults.
questions.answer:
import json

patient = json.loads(patient_json)

try:
    name_entry = patient.get('name', [{}])[0]
    full_name = f"{name_entry.get('given', [''])[0]} {name_entry.get('family', '')}"
except (IndexError, TypeError):
    full_name = 'Unknown'

birth_date = patient.get('birthDate', 'Unknown')

mrn = 'Unknown'
for identifier in patient.get('identifier', []):
    if 'mrn' in identifier.get('system', '').lower():
        mrn = identifier.get('value', 'Unknown')
        break

print(f'Name: {full_name}, DOB: {birth_date}, MRN: {mrn}')

rubric.key: Correct JSON parsing with error handling
Proper FHIR name extraction from name array
MRN extraction with system string matching
Defensive coding with .get() defaults for missing fields

questions.question: You have a pandas DataFrame of patient vital signs with columns: patient_id, date, bp_systolic, bp_diastolic, heart_rate. Describe which chart type you would use to visualize: (a) the distribution of systolic blood pressures across all patients, (b) a single patient's blood pressure trend over 12 months, (c) average heart rate comparison between male and female patients. Justify each choice.
questions.answer: (a) Histogram: shows the shape of the distribution (normal, skewed, bimodal), identifies outliers, and reveals the central tendency and spread. (b) Line chart: date on x-axis, BP on y-axis; line charts are the standard for temporal trends because they connect sequential measurements to show direction and rate of change. (c) Bar chart (or box plot): bar chart compares two group means directly; box plot shows the full distribution (median, quartiles, outliers) for each group. Box plot is preferred because it reveals whether the distributions overlap, not just whether the means differ.

rubric.key: Correct chart type for each scenario
Clear justification referencing data characteristics
Mentions practical considerations like outliers or distributions

quizzes.topic: APIs, Data Cleaning, and Statistical Analysis

questions.question: Write Python code using the requests library to query a FHIR server at 'https://fhir.example.org' for all Observation resources with LOINC code 4548-4 (A1c) for patient ID 'P123'. Parse the response Bundle to extract the date and numeric value from each observation. Include error handling for HTTP errors.
questions.answer:
import requests

url = 'https://fhir.example.org/Observation'
params = {
    'code': '4548-4',
    'patient': 'P123',
    '_sort': '-date'
}

response = requests.get(url, params=params)
response.raise_for_status()

bundle = response.json()
results = []
for entry in bundle.get('entry', []):
    obs = entry.get('resource', {})
    date = obs.get('effectiveDateTime', 'Unknown')
    value = obs.get('valueQuantity', {}).get('value', None)
    if value is not None:
        results.append({'date': date, 'a1c': value})

print(results)

rubric.key: Correct FHIR URL construction with search parameters
Proper response handling with raise_for_status()
Correct Bundle entry iteration and value extraction
Defensive coding with .get() for nested FHIR structure

questions.question: A hospital discharge dataset contains a 'temperature' column with values like 98.6, 99.1, 986, 0, -1, and NaN. Write a pandas data cleaning function that: (1) converts non-numeric entries to NaN, (2) flags values outside the physiological range 90-110 as data errors, (3) replaces flagged values with NaN, and (4) returns a count of cleaned values.
questions.answer:
def clean_temperature(df):
    df['temperature'] = pd.to_numeric(df['temperature'], errors='coerce')
    invalid_mask = (df['temperature'] < 90) | (df['temperature'] > 110)
    invalid_count = invalid_mask.sum()
    df.loc[invalid_mask, 'temperature'] = None
    return df, invalid_count

rubric.key: Correct conversion to numeric with errors='coerce'
Correct physiological range check
Proper replacement of invalid values
Returns count of cleaned values

questions.question: You run a t-test comparing length of stay between surgical and medical patients and get p=0.0001 with a mean difference of 0.2 days (4.8 hours). A colleague says 'The difference is highly significant, we need to change our staffing model.' Do you agree? Explain your reasoning, distinguishing between statistical and clinical significance.
questions.answer: The p-value of 0.0001 means the difference is statistically significant: it is very unlikely to have occurred by chance. However, the effect size of 0.2 days (4.8 hours) is clinically trivial. A staffing model change requires differences of at least 0.5-1.0 days to justify the operational cost and disruption. With a large sample size (which hospital datasets typically have), even meaningless differences become statistically significant. I would not recommend staffing changes based on a 4.8-hour average difference. Instead, I would look at the distribution (are there subgroups with larger differences?) and compute the effect size (Cohen's d) to quantify practical importance.

rubric.key: Correct identification that statistical significance does not equal clinical importance
Explains large sample size effect on p-values
Provides constructive next steps or alternative analysis

quizzes.topic: Health Standards, Data Pipelines, and SQL

questions.question: Write a Python function that takes an ICD-10-CM code string and returns its chapter-level grouping. The function should extract the first character to determine the chapter: codes starting with 'E' are 'Endocrine', 'I' are 'Circulatory', 'J' are 'Respiratory', 'K' are 'Digestive'. Return 'Other' for unrecognized prefixes. Validate that the code is at least 3 characters long.

rubric.key: Correct chapter mapping logic
Input validation for code length
Clean implementation with dictionary lookup

questions.question: Write a SQL query that finds all patients with at least 2 emergency department encounters in the last 90 days. Use tables: patients (patient_id, name) and encounters (encounter_id, patient_id, encounter_type, encounter_date). Return patient_id, name, and encounter count, ordered by count descending.
questions.answer:
SELECT p.patient_id, p.name, COUNT(e.encounter_id) AS ed_visits
FROM patients p
JOIN encounters e ON p.patient_id = e.patient_id
WHERE e.encounter_type = 'ED'
  AND e.encounter_date >= DATE('now', '-90 days')
GROUP BY p.patient_id, p.name
HAVING COUNT(e.encounter_id) >= 2
ORDER BY ed_visits DESC;

rubric.key: Correct JOIN between patients and encounters
Correct WHERE filter for type and date range
Correct GROUP BY with HAVING >= 2
Correct ORDER BY descending

questions.question: Describe three key differences between processing healthcare data in a Python script versus a manual Excel workflow. For each difference, explain why it matters for a health informatics professional working with PHI.
questions.answer: (1) Reproducibility: A Python script produces identical results every time it runs with the same input. Excel workflows depend on manual steps that may be performed differently, leading to inconsistent results. This matters for regulatory audits and quality reporting where results must be verifiable. (2) Auditability: Python code and git history document exactly what transformations were applied to the data. Excel changes are invisible once saved, making it impossible to trace how a result was derived. HIPAA requires audit trails for PHI access and processing. (3) Scalability: Python handles millions of rows efficiently; Excel becomes slow and unreliable above 100K rows. As healthcare datasets grow (claims, EHR exports, population health), Python scales while Excel does not. Additionally, Python scripts can be scheduled to run automatically, reducing manual PHI handling.

rubric.key: Three distinct, valid differences identified
Each connected to health informatics professional context
Mentions PHI, compliance, or regulatory considerations

quizzes.topic: Privacy, Advanced Analysis, and Comprehensive Review

questions.question: Write a Python function that de-identifies a patient DataFrame according to HIPAA Safe Harbor. The DataFrame has columns: name, dob, zip_code, mrn, diagnosis, los. The function should: remove name, convert dob to birth year only, truncate zip to 3 digits, hash mrn with SHA-256, and keep diagnosis and los unchanged.
questions.answer:
import hashlib
import pandas as pd

def deidentify(df):
    result = df.copy()
    result = result.drop(columns=['name'])
    result['birth_year'] = pd.to_datetime(result['dob']).dt.year
    result = result.drop(columns=['dob'])
    result['zip_3'] = result['zip_code'].astype(str).str[:3]
    result = result.drop(columns=['zip_code'])
    result['pseudo_id'] = result['mrn'].apply(
        lambda x: hashlib.sha256(str(x).encode()).hexdigest()[:12]
    )
    result = result.drop(columns=['mrn'])
    return result

rubric.key: Correct removal of direct identifiers (name)
Correct date generalization to year only
Correct zip code truncation to 3 digits
Correct MRN hashing with SHA-256
Preserves non-PHI columns unchanged

questions.question: Write pandas code to create a pivot table from a discharge DataFrame showing average length of stay with rows as primary_diagnosis, columns as discharge_quarter (Q1-Q4), and values as mean los. Add a 'Total' column showing the overall average for each diagnosis. Filter to show only diagnoses with total average LOS > 5 days.
questions.answer:
pivot = pd.pivot_table(
    df,
    values='los',
    index='primary_diagnosis',
    columns='discharge_quarter',
    aggfunc='mean'
)
pivot['Total'] = df.groupby('primary_diagnosis')['los'].mean()
pivot = pivot[pivot['Total'] > 5]
pivot = pivot.sort_values('Total', ascending=False)
print(pivot.round(1))

rubric.key: Correct pivot_table construction with appropriate arguments
Correct Total column calculation
Correct filtering by Total > 5
Appropriate sorting and formatting

questions.question: You are asked to build a Python data pipeline that runs daily to process new lab result files from a hospital LIS. Describe the five main steps your pipeline should include, what could go wrong at each step, and how you would handle each failure mode.
questions.answer: (1) File Discovery: Scan input directory for new CSV files. Failure: no new files (log and exit cleanly), permission error (log error, alert admin). (2) Data Reading and Validation: Read each CSV, validate column names and data types. Failure: malformed CSV (log file name, skip, alert), unexpected columns (reject file, alert source system team). (3) Data Cleaning: Apply range checks on lab values, standardize LOINC codes, handle missing values. Failure: unusually high error rate (>10% invalid records suggests source system problem; halt and alert rather than producing a misleading report). (4) Analysis and Reporting: Compute daily lab statistics, flag critical values, generate summary. Failure: empty dataset after cleaning (report on data quality issue rather than producing empty report). (5) Output and Archiving: Write report to output directory, archive processed input files. Failure: disk full (log error, alert), write permission error (retry to alternate location). All steps log to a centralized log file with timestamps for debugging.

rubric.key: Five logical pipeline steps identified
Realistic failure modes for each step
Appropriate error handling strategies
Mentions logging and alerting

major_assignments.title: Healthcare Data Analysis Pipeline
major_assignments.description: Build a complete Python data analysis pipeline that reads a healthcare dataset (hospital discharges, lab results, or clinical registry data), performs thorough data quality assessment, cleans and validates the data, conducts at least five clinically meaningful analyses using pandas, produces professional visualizations, and writes a narrative interpretation of findings. The pipeline must handle real-world data quality issues gracefully and produce a reproducible Jupyter notebook that another analyst could run on similar data.
objectives: Apply Python file I/O and pandas to a realistic health data analysis task

Another analyst could reproduce the analysis from the notebook alone

major_assignments.title: Automated Healthcare Quality Reporting System
major_assignments.description: Build a complete automated healthcare quality reporting system that: (1) reads data from multiple sources (CSV files, a SQLite database, and optionally a FHIR API), (2) applies data quality checks and cleaning, (3) computes standard healthcare quality metrics (readmission rates, average LOS, mortality rates, infection rates) by unit, provider, and time period, (4) performs de-identification for any PHI in the output, (5) generates a multi-page report with executive summary, detailed metrics, trend charts, and statistical comparisons, and (6) exports results to Excel and PDF formats. This capstone project demonstrates mastery of all course concepts in a single integrated application.

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes assessing programming concepts, pandas skills, and health informatics knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Weekly coding exercises applying programming to health informatics scenarios with increasing complexity

major_assignments.description: Three major projects: data analysis pipeline, FHIR API integration, and automated quality reporting system

midterm_examination.description: Practical coding exam covering Python fundamentals, data structures, file processing, and basic pandas

final_project.description: Capstone quality reporting system demonstrating mastery of all course concepts in an integrated application

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Programming is a skill built through practice with instructor support. More than 2 unexcused lab absences result in a 5% grade reduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late code submissions lose 10% per day for up to 5 days. All code must run without errors to receive credit. Extensions granted for documented emergencies only.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of programming concepts and debugging approaches is encouraged. All code must be individually written. Identical code submissions are treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be the student's own work. Use of AI coding assistants must be disclosed and the student must explain every line of generated code. Copying code without attribution is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with Anaconda Python, Jupyter notebooks, and VS Code. All software is free and cross-platform. Students must bring their laptop to every lab session.
